human_use.rationale: The Artifact is a human-operated mobile checkout. Its success depends on discoverability, navigation, input burden, error recovery, feedback, accessibility, touch ergonomics, interruption recovery, perceived latency, destructive-action safety, and cognitive load across cross-disability use.

objective_gate_note: All seven objective gates pass per generated/judging/blind-loop-01/candidate-b/evidence/objective-report.json. Qualitative human-use scores are not objective gates and are not used to override them.

lens_findings.discoverability: Site header, banner disclosing synthetic scope, and an in-page 'Checkout sections' nav (Contact/Delivery/Shipping/Payment/Review) as anchor pills. Numbered section circles reinforce the 5-step arc. Order summary card at the top of the flow with running total. Payment section carries a 'Fictional card to use' fixture panel that reveals the exact demo values. Evidence: screenshots/candidate-b/01-initial-390x844.png; transcript audit landmarks/headings extra (nav:1, aside:1, main:1).
lens_findings.navigation: Anchor pills jump between sections; Edit contact/Edit shipping buttons call scrollIntoView + focus the first field of the target section and narrate the move. First Tab focus lands on the section-nav 'Contact' anchor (transcript 'keyboard: first Tab focus'). Missing a true skip-link, but the section-nav row functions similarly for sighted keyboard users; screen-reader users get a labelled nav landmark.
lens_findings.input_burden: Validation is strict-fixture: state must equal 'NSW' (case-sensitive), country must equal 'australia' (lowercased); card number must equal '4111111111111111'; expiry must equal '12/34'; CSC must equal '123'; cardholder must equal 'alex morgan'. Any real-world variation is rejected. All inputs also have autocomplete='off', which disables browser autofill / password-manager assistance — a documented accessibility anti-pattern for motor-constrained and cognitively-impaired users. The 'Synthetic value:' helper text and fixture-card do mitigate for sighted users who read them, but the underlying interaction burden is high. Evidence: candidate-b/app.js fieldRules + errorMessages, candidate-b/index.html autocomplete='off' on every input.
lens_findings.error_prevention_recovery: Empty submit produced 13 highly specific error messages routed through a single error summary that focuses itself and lists each item as a link ('Enter the synthetic phone number with at least 8 digits.', 'Select NSW for this demonstration.', 'Enter the 4-digit synthetic postcode.'). aria-invalid + aria-describedby chained to the correct help+error IDs; on correction each field's error is cleared on input/change. Evidence: transcript 'post-invalid-submit report' errorSummaryVisible=true, invalidsCount ~13.
lens_findings.feedback_status: Polite aria-live status region with aria-atomic='true' updates through the flow: 'Safe contact, delivery and shipping progress saved…', 'Placing local synthetic order. Please wait.', 'Synthetic order placed once. Confirmation ID SYN-2048.' Assertive live on the error summary. Focus moves to confirmation section on completion (transcript 'confirmation state' activeElement=SECTION).
lens_findings.accessibility: Solid landmark tree (header/nav/main/aside/footer), fieldset+legend per section, aria-describedby chained, error summary role='alert' with tabindex=-1 that receives focus after invalid submit. Concerns: (a) Shipping uses a double-radio anti-pattern — <label class='choice-card' role='radio' aria-checked> wrapping a real <input type='radio' aria-hidden='true'> — screen-reader users may hear the group twice or miss state updates because aria-checked is set only via the keydown handler, not by the input's own change; (b) autocomplete='off' on every field is an anti-pattern for AT-assisted form filling; (c) no visible skip-link even though the section-nav helps sighted keyboard users. Evidence: candidate-b/index.html lines 171–184; candidate-b/app.js keydown handler on shipping-* elements.
lens_findings.responsive_touch_ergonomics: At 320x568 no horizontal scroll (transcript '320x568: viewport initial' hasHorizontalScroll=false). Shipping choice-cards and Place-order button are large tap targets; section anchors are pill-sized. Mobile-touch objective gate passes independently.
lens_findings.interruption_resumption: Safe fields (contact + delivery + shipping) are auto-persisted on input; on reload they are restored and the status region announces 'Safe progress restored. Re-enter synthetic payment details; they were not saved.' Reload persistence check (transcript) shows payment: {card-*: '' } across all four payment fields. No manual save or clear controls — users cannot deliberately discard progress without clearing site data.
lens_findings.latency_perception: 180 ms placement setTimeout, placeOrder.disabled=true immediately, aria-busy='true' during processing, then narrated 'Synthetic order placed once. Confirmation ID SYN-2048.' Perceived latency is short.
lens_findings.destructive_actions: No user-facing destructive control beyond Place order itself. Place order is guarded by a placementStarted flag and disabled after success; a second Enter/click after completion re-narrates 'This synthetic order is already confirmed as SYN-2048. No duplicate was created.' Transcript 'duplicate-safety report' confIds: ['SYN-2048'], placeOrderDisabled=true.
lens_findings.cognitive_load: All five sections are on one continuously scrolled page. Nav pills help orientation, but ~20 required fields plus fixture cards plus a review block are visible at once, which is heavy on a small viewport. The exact-match validation adds hidden rules ('why won't it accept 4111-1111-1111-1111 with dashes?') that a real cross-disability user must reason through. The review block is helpful but reprints only aggregate values rather than every field verbatim, so users must scroll back to correct.

strengths: Error messages are among the most specific and actionable observed — they name the exact field and expected format.
aria-live status region combined with focused error summary gives strong feedback loops on both success and failure.
Section-nav anchor pills give sighted keyboard users a fast way to jump around the long single-page flow.
Duplicate submit is guarded twice (placementStarted flag + disabled button + status re-announce).

defects.finding: Exact-fixture validation (state must equal 'NSW', country 'australia', card '4111111111111111', name 'alex morgan', etc.) turns the checkout into a memory test of the fictional values. Real cross-disability users, especially those with cognitive impairment, will hit rejection loops.
autocomplete='off' on every input disables browser and AT autofill — a documented anti-pattern that penalises motor-constrained and cognitively-impaired users.
Custom role='radio' on <label> elements wrapping aria-hidden real radios (double-radio pattern) creates ambiguity for screen-readers and relies on a keydown handler to update aria-checked.
All five sections and ~20 required fields load in one continuous scroll; the review block does not reprint every entered value verbatim.

uncertainty: Blinded evidence; Track paradigm inferred from artifact behaviour (single-page landmarked checkout).
Screen-reader announcement fidelity of the double-radio pattern is inferred from ARIA and DOM structure, not measured with an actual AT.

loop_02_recommendation.change: Replace the double-radio shipping choice-card pattern with a plain <fieldset><legend>+ real <input type='radio'> pairs styled as cards, remove autocomplete='off' from every input, and relax the input validation to shape-only checks (email regex, 4-digit postcode, 16-digit numeric card ignoring spaces, MM/YY, 3-digit CSC) — keep the Synthetic-value hints for guidance, but accept legitimate variations so cross-disability users are not blocked by exact-match rules.
